title: ABC谋杀案
summary:
Officials, including the Deputy Commissioner, Assistant Commissioner, Dr. Thompson, Inspector Croft, and Hercule Poirot, meet to discuss whether to disclose the contents of anonymous letters to the media. They note that the Bexhill murder case has garnered significantly more public attention than the Andree-Wolfe case because the victim was a young woman and the crime occurred at a popular seaside resort. Public interest has focused on the ABC train schedule, leading to a theory that the killer travels by train.

The group debates the consequences of publicity. Croft and Poirot argue that the killer seeks infamy and notoriety, and making the case public would satisfy this desire. Dr. Thompson suggests that regardless of whether the police conduct a secret or public investigation, the killer will likely commit another crime to force their hand. Poirot speculates that the killer intends to follow the alphabet from A to Z, though he believes the killer will be caught before reaching the end. Inspector Croft expresses confidence that the criminal will be caught before the letter F, theorizing that overconfidence and growing carelessness will lead to mistakes.

Regarding the Bexhill case, Croft reports that a waiter at Sprand's in Eastbourne saw the victim dining with a middle-aged man in glasses on the 24th, and another witness saw her with a man resembling a naval officer near the Red Kidney Beans pub that same evening. Poirot emphasizes the need to find a motive beyond alphabetical obsession. While Dr. Thompson notes that both victims are women, Poirot questions if the killer is acting out of personal amusement, a specific psychological need, or a personal hatred of him due to his foreign nationality or a past defeat.
